section.title: The Eastern Tribes Return
section.sectionRationale: With their duty done, the eastern tribes are blessed and sent home across the Jordan. On the way they build a huge altar, and the western tribes, fearing apostasy, muster for war. But before striking, they send a delegation to ask, and the eastern tribes explain: the altar is not for sacrifice but a witness that they too belong to the LORD. War is averted, and the altar is named Witness. It is one movement, a near-tragedy over worship that becomes a testimony to the unity of God's people and their jealousy for pure worship.
section.thesis: Zeal for the purity of worship, one altar before the one God, nearly brings the tribes to war, but because they go and ask rather than assume the worst, a misunderstanding is cleared, revealing that the eastern tribes built not a rival altar but a witness to their shared portion in the LORD, so that both the jealousy for right worship and the recourse to honest inquiry are vindicated.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the eastern tribes blessed and sent home, verses 1 to 9; the altar built and Israel's alarm, verses 10 to 12; the delegation's charge of rebellion, verses 13 to 20; the eastern tribes' explanation, verses 21 to 29; and the resolution and naming of the altar, verses 30 to 34.

units.label: The eastern tribes blessed and sent home
units.note: you have kept the charge of the LORD; love Him with all your heart

verses.text: Then Joshua summoned the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh
and told them, “You have done all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you, and you have obeyed my voice in all that I commanded you.
All this time you have not deserted your brothers, up to this very day, but have kept the charge given you by the LORD your God.
And now that the LORD your God has given your brothers rest as He promised them, you may return to your homes in the land that Moses the servant of the LORD gave you across the Jordan.

But be very careful to observe the commandment and the law that Moses the servant of the LORD gave you: to love the LORD your God, to walk in all His ways, to keep His commandments, to hold fast to Him, and to serve Him with all your heart and with all your soul.”
So Joshua blessed them and sent them on their way, and they went to their homes.

(To the half-tribe of Manasseh Moses had given land in Bashan, and to the other half Joshua gave land on the west side of the Jordan among their brothers.) When Joshua sent them to their homes he blessed them,
saying, “Return to your homes with your great wealth, with immense herds of livestock, with silver, gold, bronze, iron, and very many clothes. Divide with your brothers the spoil of your enemies.”

units.label: The altar built and Israel's alarm
units.note: the whole congregation assembled to go to war

verses.text: So the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh left the Israelites at Shiloh in the land of Canaan to return to their own land of Gilead, which they had acquired according to the command of the LORD through Moses.
And when they came to Geliloth near the Jordan in the land of Canaan, the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh built an imposing {{mizbeach|altar}} there by the Jordan.

Then the Israelites received the report: “Behold, the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar on the border of the land of Canaan, at Geliloth near the Jordan on the Israelite side.”
And when they heard this, the whole congregation of Israel assembled at Shiloh to go to war against them.

units.label: The delegation's charge of rebellion
units.note: what is this breach of faith against the God of Israel

verses.text: The Israelites sent Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest to the land of Gilead, to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
With him they sent ten chiefs, one family leader from each tribe of Israel, each the head of a family among the clans of Israel.
They went to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in the land of Gilead and said to them,

“This is what the whole congregation of the LORD says: ‘What is this {{maal|breach of faith}} you have committed today against the God of Israel by turning away from the LORD and building for yourselves an altar, that you might rebel against the LORD this day?
Was not the sin of Peor enough for us, from which we have not cleansed ourselves to this day? It even brought a plague upon the congregation of the LORD.
And now, would you turn away from the LORD? If you rebel today against the LORD, tomorrow He will be angry with the whole congregation of Israel.

If indeed the land of your inheritance is unclean, then cross over to the land of the LORD’s possession, where the LORD’s tabernacle stands, and take possession of it among us. But do not rebel against the LORD or against us by building for yourselves an altar other than the altar of the LORD our God.
Was not Achan son of Zerah unfaithful regarding what was set apart for destruction, bringing wrath upon the whole congregation of Israel? Yet it was not only Achan who perished because of his sin!’”

units.label: The eastern tribes' explanation
units.note: let it be a witness between us that the LORD is God

verses.text: Then the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh answered the leaders of the clans of Israel:
“The LORD, the Mighty One, is God! The LORD, the Mighty One, is God! He knows, and may Israel also know. If this was in rebellion or breach of faith against the LORD, do not spare us today.
If we have built for ourselves an altar to turn away from Him and to offer burnt offerings and grain offerings on it, or to sacrifice peace offerings on it, may the LORD Himself hold us accountable.
But in fact we have done this for fear that in the future your descendants might say to ours, ‘What have you to do with the LORD, the God of Israel?
For the LORD has made the Jordan a border between us and you Reubenites and Gadites. You have no share in the LORD!’ So your descendants could cause ours to stop fearing the LORD.
That is why we said, ‘Let us take action and build an altar for ourselves, but not for burnt offerings or sacrifices.

Rather, let it be a {{ed|witness}} between us and you and the generations to come, that we will worship the LORD in His presence with our burnt offerings, sacrifices, and peace offerings.’ Then in the future, your descendants cannot say to ours, ‘You have no share in the LORD!’

Therefore we said, ‘If they ever say this to us or to our descendants, we will answer: Look at the replica of the altar of the LORD that our fathers made, not for burnt offerings or sacrifices, but as a witness between us and you.’

Far be it from us to rebel against the LORD and turn away from Him today by building an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices, other than the altar of the LORD our God, which stands before His tabernacle.”

units.label: The resolution and the altar named Witness
units.note: today we know that the LORD is among us

verses.text: When Phinehas the priest and the chiefs of the congregation, the heads of Israel’s clans who were with him, heard what the descendants of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh had to say, they were satisfied.

Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest said to the descendants of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh, “Today we know that the LORD is among us, because you have not committed this breach of faith against Him. Consequently, you have delivered the Israelites from the hand of the LORD.”

Then Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest, together with the other leaders, returned to the Israelites in the land of Canaan and brought back a report regarding the Reubenites and Gadites in the land of Gilead.
The Israelites were satisfied with the report, and they blessed God and spoke no more about going to war against them to destroy the land where the Reubenites and Gadites lived.
So the Reubenites and Gadites named the altar Witness, for they said, “It is a witness between us that the LORD is God.”

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, the place of sacrifice. The huge mizbeach the eastern tribes built alarmed the rest, because sacrifice was to be offered at only one altar, before the LORD's tabernacle, Deuteronomy 12. A second altar for sacrifice would have been rank apostasy; the whole crisis turns on the conviction that there is one place and one altar for worship of the one God, a truth that points to Christ, our one altar and one sacrifice.

maal.gloss: A treacherous breach of faith, an act of unfaithfulness, especially against holy things. The western tribes fear the altar is a maal against the LORD, the very word used of Achan's sin, Joshua 7:1. Their alarm shows how seriously they took unfaithfulness in worship, having learned from Achan and Peor that one group's sin could bring wrath on the whole congregation.

ed.gloss: A witness, testimony. The altar is named Ed, Witness, because it testifies that the eastern tribes, though separated by the Jordan, share in the LORD and His worship. Not a place of sacrifice but a memorial, it guards against a future generation being told they have no part in the God of Israel; it stands as a lasting testimony to the unity of God's people.

basic.context: The eastern tribes, Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh, have kept their promise to fight alongside their brothers until the land was won. Now Joshua commends them, urges them to love and serve the LORD wholeheartedly, and sends them home across the Jordan with a blessing and much spoil. But on the way they build a huge altar by the Jordan. When the western tribes hear of it, they assume the worst, a rival altar means apostasy, which could bring God's wrath on everyone, so they gather for war. Wisely, though, they first send a delegation led by Phinehas the priest to ask what is going on. The eastern tribes explain: the altar is not for sacrifice at all, but a witness, so that future generations will know they too belong to the LORD despite the river between them. Everyone is satisfied, war is averted, and the altar is named Witness. The question underneath is: how do God's people guard pure worship and preserve unity without tearing each other apart?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative with a clear lesson. It affirms zeal for the purity of worship, one altar before the one God, and it models the wisdom of inquiry over assumption: the western tribes almost went to war on a false conclusion, but asking first uncovered the truth. The altar as a witness also speaks to preserving the unity of God's people across dividing lines. We should learn both the jealousy for right worship and the grace of not judging our brothers rashly.
basic.originalLanguages: The crisis centers on the mizbeach, the altar, because there was to be only one altar for sacrifice. The western tribes feared a maal, a breach of faith, the same word used of Achan. But the altar was really an ed, a witness, testifying that the eastern tribes shared in the LORD.
basic.typology: The insistence on one altar before the one God points to Christ, our one altar and one final sacrifice, at whom alone all God's people worship. The witness altar preserving the unity of the tribes across the Jordan foreshadows the one people of God, near and far, united in Christ.
basic.christ: The whole crisis rests on the truth that there is one altar and one place of sacrifice, which points to Christ: we have an altar, and He is the one sacrifice for sins for all time, Hebrews 13:10 and 10:12. And the witness altar, ensuring that those across the river still shared in the LORD, foreshadows how Christ makes one people of those near and far, breaking down every dividing wall.
basic.god: God is the one God who is to be worshiped in one way, at one altar, and the tribes' jealousy for this honors Him. He is the God who is among His people, and whose wrath against unfaithfulness is real, as Achan and Peor showed. And He is the God of the whole people, east and west, so that none may be told they have no share in Him.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 12 commands one place of sacrifice. Numbers 25 recounts the sin of Peor referenced here. Hebrews 13 says we have an altar, Christ. And Ephesians 2 shows Christ making one people of those near and far.
basic.oneStory: The eastern tribes' witness altar nearly sparks a civil war over the purity of worship, but honest inquiry reveals it as a testimony to their shared portion in the LORD. The episode upholds one altar before the one God, pointing to Christ our one sacrifice, and preserves the unity of God's people across the Jordan, foreshadowing the one people united in Him.

context.literary: Chapter 22 begins the closing section of Joshua, resolving the loose end of the eastern tribes. It is a tense, well-told narrative that moves from commendation to crisis to resolution, with long speeches at its heart. It deliberately recalls earlier episodes of communal guilt, Achan in chapter 7 and Peor in Numbers 25, to explain the western tribes' alarm. The naming of the altar Witness at the end echoes other memorial names in Joshua and ties the resolution to the theme of testimony for future generations.
context.historical: Under the law, sacrifice was to be offered only at the central sanctuary, then at Shiloh, Deuteronomy 12:5-14; building a second altar for sacrifice would have been a grave act of apostasy. The Jordan formed a natural and, the eastern tribes feared, a potential religious barrier between them and the rest of Israel. Phinehas, who had zealously stopped the plague at Peor, Numbers 25:7-13, was the fitting leader for a delegation about worship. The willingness to send envoys and ask before attacking reflected the law's own concern to investigate before acting against a supposed idolatrous city, Deuteronomy 13:12-15.
context.canonical: The episode upholds the biblical principle of one place and one altar for the worship of the one God, which finds its fulfillment in Christ, the one and final sacrifice, so that we have an altar in Him, Hebrews 13:10. The concern that the eastern tribes not be cut off from a share in the LORD anticipates the New Testament breaking down of dividing walls, making one people of those near and far in Christ, Ephesians 2:13-14. The chapter's model of inquiry before judgment resonates with the wisdom and gospel call not to judge rashly but to seek the truth, Proverbs 18:13 and John 7:24.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To resolve the account of the eastern tribes by showing their faithfulness, to record the near-conflict over the altar and its peaceful resolution, and to affirm both zeal for the purity of worship at the one altar and the wisdom of inquiry that preserved the unity of God's people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific altar dispute belongs to Israel's covenant life under the one-sanctuary law. What endures is the twofold lesson: God's people should be jealous for pure worship and alert to unfaithfulness, and they should seek the truth and not assume the worst of one another, guarding both holiness and unity.

debates.question: Were the western tribes right to muster for war over the altar?

views.label: Their zeal was right, even if their conclusion was premature
views.case: They had learned from Achan and Peor that one group's unfaithfulness could bring God's wrath on all Israel, so their jealousy for pure worship and their readiness to act against apostasy were commendable; crucially, they sent a delegation to investigate before striking, which is exactly what the law required, so their zeal was tempered by due process.

views.label: They came dangerously close to a rash judgment
views.case: They assumed the worst and gathered for war before knowing the facts, nearly shedding their brothers' blood over a misunderstanding; the narrative implicitly warns against judging by appearances, and it is only the decision to ask first that averts tragedy, so the episode cautions against rash conclusions even when motivated by zeal.

debates.bearing: Both together give the balance the chapter commends: be genuinely jealous for pure worship and alert to sin, yet investigate and seek the truth before condemning a brother, so that holiness and unity are both preserved.

study.originalLanguages: Three words frame the drama. The crisis is provoked by a mizbeach, an altar, and everything hangs on the fact that under the law there was to be only one altar for sacrifice, before the LORD's tabernacle, Deuteronomy 12; a second sacrificial altar would be apostasy, so the sight of a great altar by the Jordan set off alarm. The western tribes name their fear precisely: they suspect a maal, a treacherous breach of faith against the LORD, the very word used of Achan's sacrilege in chapter 7, and they remember that such unfaithfulness brought wrath on the whole congregation. But the eastern tribes reveal the altar's true purpose with a third word: it is an ed, a witness. Not a place of sacrifice at all, but a memorial, a standing testimony that they, though the Jordan divides them from the tabernacle, still share in the LORD and His worship, so that no future generation could tell their children, you have no part in the God of Israel. Altar, breach of faith, witness: the vocabulary moves from suspected apostasy to vindicated unity, and it turns the near-tragedy into a monument of shared faith.

typology.type: The one altar before the one God, guarded so jealously
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our one altar and one final sacrifice for sins, at whom alone God's people worship, Hebrews 13:10 and 10:12-14.

typology.type: The witness altar preserving the eastern tribes' share in the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Christ making one people of those near and far, breaking down the dividing wall, Ephesians 2:13-14.

typology.type: Inquiry that seeks the truth before condemning a brother
typology.fulfillment: The gospel call to judge with right judgment and not by appearances, and to guard the unity of the Spirit, John 7:24 and Ephesians 4:3.

study.christ: This near-war turns on a truth that runs straight to Christ: there is one altar, and sacrifice belongs there alone. Under the law, that meant the single altar before the tabernacle; to build another for sacrifice would be to fracture the worship of the one God. The New Testament tells us where that one altar finally stands: we have an altar from which those who serve the tabernacle have no right to eat, Hebrews 13:10, for Christ offered a single sacrifice for sins for all time and sat down at the right hand of God, Hebrews 10:12. All God's people, in every place, worship at that one altar, in Him. And the witness altar itself preaches Christ in another way. Its whole purpose was to make sure that those separated by the Jordan would never be told, you have no share in the LORD. That is exactly what Christ secures: you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ, for He Himself is our peace, who has made the two one and broken down the dividing wall, Ephesians 2:13-14. The chapter's jealousy for one altar and its care for the far-off tribe both find their Yes in the one Savior who unites one people in Himself.
study.god: The God of this chapter is one, holy, present, and jealous for right worship, yet also the God of the whole people. He is one, and He is to be worshiped in one way at one altar, so that a rival altar would be an intolerable breach; the tribes' fierce concern honors His exclusive claim. He is holy and just, and the memory of Achan and Peor reminds all Israel that unfaithfulness in worship is no small thing and can draw down His wrath on the community. He is present among His people, as Phinehas confesses, today we know that the LORD is among us. And He is the God of the whole people, east and west, near and far, unwilling that any who belong to Him should be told they have no share in Him. This God calls His people to guard His worship jealously and to guard their unity carefully, both for His glory.

crossReferences.note: Sacrifice was to be offered only at the one place the LORD would choose, the law behind the alarm over a second altar.
The sin of Peor and the plague, cited by the delegation as a warning of communal guilt for unfaithfulness.
Achan's breach of faith brought wrath on all Israel, the precedent the western tribes feared repeating.
We have an altar, Christ, the one and final place of sacrifice for God's people.
Christ brings the far-off near and makes one people, the unity the witness altar sought to preserve.

study.oneStory: The eastern tribes' witness altar nearly ignites a civil war over the purity of worship, but because the western tribes send envoys to ask rather than strike in haste, the truth comes out: the altar is not a rival place of sacrifice but a testimony that those across the Jordan still share in the LORD. The episode upholds one altar before the one God, pointing to Christ our one sacrifice, and preserves the unity of God's people across the dividing river, foreshadowing the one people, near and far, united forever in Him.

quiz.q: Why did the western tribes prepare to go to war against the eastern tribes?

options.text: They feared the great altar by the Jordan was an act of rebellion and apostasy against the LORD.
options.note: Verses 10 to 12 and 16 show their fear that the altar meant unfaithful worship.

options.text: The eastern tribes had refused to share the spoil of war.
options.note: The eastern tribes were sent home with spoil to share; the alarm was over the altar, not spoil.

options.text: The eastern tribes had seized land west of the Jordan.
options.note: The dispute was about the altar and worship, not a land grab.

options.text: The eastern tribes had attacked Shiloh.
options.note: There was no attack; the western tribes feared the altar signaled apostasy.

quiz.q: What did the western tribes do before actually attacking?

options.text: They sent a delegation led by Phinehas to ask the eastern tribes what the altar meant.
options.note: Verses 13 to 15 show they inquired first, which uncovered the truth.

options.text: They attacked immediately without any warning.
options.note: They gathered for war but wisely sent envoys to ask before striking.

options.text: They tore down the altar in secret.
options.note: They did not destroy the altar; they sent a delegation to investigate.

options.text: They appealed to a foreign king to judge the matter.
options.note: They sent their own delegation of the priest and chiefs, not a foreign king.

quiz.q: What was the true purpose of the altar the eastern tribes built?

options.text: A witness that they too shared in the LORD, not a place for sacrifice.
options.note: Verses 26 to 28 explain the altar was a memorial witness, not for offerings.

options.text: A rival place to offer sacrifices closer to home.
options.note: The opposite: they explicitly built it not for sacrifice, but as a witness.

options.text: A monument boasting of their wealth and spoil.
options.note: It was a witness to their share in the LORD, not a boast of wealth.

options.text: A shrine to a foreign god.
options.note: It was a witness to the LORD, the God of Israel, not to any foreign god.

quiz.q: What twofold lesson does this episode teach God's people?

options.text: Be jealous for pure worship and alert to unfaithfulness, yet seek the truth before condemning a brother.
options.note: The chapter commends both zeal for right worship and inquiry that averted a rash war.

options.text: Always assume the worst about others and act on it.
options.note: The opposite: the crisis was averted precisely because they asked instead of assuming.

options.text: Worship does not really matter to God.
options.note: Worship mattered intensely; the whole crisis arose from zeal for pure worship.

options.text: Unity should be kept even at the cost of tolerating idolatry.
options.note: The tribes would not tolerate idolatry; unity was preserved because there was in fact no idolatry.

questions: The western tribes were zealous for pure worship, but almost went to war on a false assumption. When have you jumped to the worst conclusion about someone before knowing the facts?
A near-tragedy was avoided simply because they asked before acting. How could seeking the truth and asking honest questions defuse a conflict you are facing right now?
The eastern tribes cared deeply that future generations would know they belonged to the LORD. What are you doing to make sure the next generation knows they have a share in God?
The whole crisis assumed there is one altar and one place of true worship, which points to Christ, our one sacrifice. How does resting in Christ as the one and final sacrifice shape your worship?
The witness altar preserved the unity of God's people across a dividing river. Where is God calling you to work for unity across the things that divide His people today?

translationNotes.note: The BSB sets off one family leader from each tribe with an em dash; this, and the similar em dashes in verse 30, have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
Achan's unfaithfulness renders forms of maal and cherem, as the BSB footnotes note; the reference recalls Joshua 7, where one man's sin brought wrath on all Israel.
The BSB footnote notes the Hebrew literally reads named the altar, for; the word Witness supplies the name the eastern tribes gave it, an altar of testimony, not sacrifice.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 22:1-34. Prose narrative with long speeches. The em dashes in verses 14 and 30 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
